draft.introduction: Albertus Magnus’s Summa Theologiae (c. 1270) represents the Dominican master’s systematic theology. Albert was Thomas Aquinas’s teacher and the first to integrate Aristotelian philosophy fully into Christian theology, earning the title Doctor Universalis.

translation.text: Theology is a science, and indeed the highest science, because it treats of the highest cause and the ultimate end of all things. It is also a wisdom, because it orders all other sciences to their proper end. And it is subalternate to no other science, since its principles are received from the divine light itself.
translation.original_text: [Latin: Theologia est scientia, et quidem scientia altissima]

translator_notes.note: Albert’s classification of theology as a “science” reflects the Aristotelian framework he pioneered. For Aristotle, a science is a body of knowledge derived from first principles through demonstration. Albert argues that theology’s first principles (articles of faith) are received from God rather than discovered by reason—making it a science dependent on divine revelation rather than natural inquiry.
